intro: The AI industry is facing a credibility check from multiple angles today — from a UN warning that AI's uneven spread could deepen global inequality, to a Tripadvisor investigation revealing that AI-generated review summaries are actively burying serious safety complaints like food poisoning and sexual harassment. Meanwhile, the hardware race heats up with SpaceX reportedly prototyping a sleek AI device to rival OpenAI's Jony Ive collaboration, and an Indian entrepreneur is betting $30 million that the entire concept of office software needs to be rebuilt from scratch for the AI era.

stories.title: v2.1.198
stories.summary: This changelog entry for Claude Code v2.1.198 describes several new features: Chrome availability becoming general, background agent notifications in the agents interface with hooks for input needs and completion, a new /dataviz skill for chart and dashboard design with color palette validation, gateway improvements adding Claude Platform on AWS as an upstream provider with failover chain enhancements, and background agent improvements launched from the agents interface. The entry appears truncated.
stories.opinion: This is a fairly standard changelog entry that covers a mix of user-facing features and infrastructure improvements. The background agent notifications and /dataviz skill seem like meaningful additions. The gateway failover chain improvement is a solid reliability enhancement. However, the entry is clearly truncated, cutting off mid-sentence, which makes it impossible to fully evaluate all the changes. The features mentioned suggest continued maturation of the Claude Code platform with better m…

stories.title: Indian tech tycoon bets $30M of his own money to build AI alternative to Microsoft Office
stories.summary: Indian serial entrepreneur Bhavin Turakhia is investing $30 million of his own money into Neo, a new enterprise work platform designed from the ground up for AI, aiming to compete with Microsoft Office and Google Apps. Launched internally in April 2026, Neo combines project management, documents, file storage, and AI into a single product. Turakhia argues that existing workplace software built before the AI era cannot simply be retrofitted with chatbots and must be redesigned entirely. The platform is model-agnostic, allowing enterprises to switch between AI models. Neo was built in three months with a team of 18 engineers using AI-assisted development, and the Bengaluru-based startup plans to expand from 45 to 100 employees by year-end while targeting mid-sized businesses in technology, consulting, and professional services.
stories.opinion: Turakhia's thesis that pre-AI workplace software needs to be rebuilt from scratch rather than patched with chatbots is intellectually compelling, and his Nokia-to-iPhone analogy resonates. However, the $30 million self-funded bet, while impressive for personal conviction, is modest compared to the billions Microsoft, Google, and others are pouring into AI-powered workplace tools. The structural challenge is enormous: enterprise software adoption depends heavily on ecosystem lock-in, data migrat…

stories.title: Are AI companies getting away with crime? | Fiona Katauskas
stories.summary: This is a Guardian editorial cartoon by Fiona Katauskas published on July 2, 2026, addressing the question of whether AI companies are getting away with crime. The cartoon's central theme is that AI companies are 'making an art of stealing intellectual property,' highlighting concerns about AI firms using copyrighted and protected creative works without permission or compensation to train their models.
stories.opinion: The cartoon takes a clear critical stance against AI companies, framing their use of creators' intellectual property as theft. By using the wordplay 'making an art of stealing,' the cartoonist draws attention to the irony that AI companies are profiting from artistic and creative works while potentially undermining the rights and livelihoods of the original creators. The piece reflects a growing sentiment among artists, writers, and other content creators that current AI training practices cons…

stories.title: AI summaries of Tripadvisor hotel reviews downplay serious complaints, investigation finds
stories.summary: An investigation found that AI-generated summaries of Tripadvisor hotel reviews systematically downplay serious complaints. Examples include describing a hotel being sued for mass food poisonings as 'spotless' and praising 'friendly' service at a resort where guests reported sexual harassment by staff. The AI overviews glossed over significant issues including mould, lack of water, and other serious concerns raised in customer reviews.
stories.opinion: This is a deeply concerning but unsurprising finding that highlights a fundamental problem with using AI to summarize user reviews. AI summary systems tend to gravitate toward averaging sentiment, which inherently dilutes extreme negative experiences — the very experiences that are most critical for consumer safety. When an AI describes a hotel facing food poisoning lawsuits as 'spotless,' it's not just an error; it's potentially dangerous misinformation that could put travelers at risk. This r…

stories.title: T-Mobile moving tens of thousands of virtual machines off VMware amid lawsuit
stories.summary: T-Mobile is suing Broadcom in New York court, seeking a ruling that Broadcom was contractually obligated to continue supporting its VMware perpetual licenses. T-Mobile purchased perpetual VMware licenses in 2023 with two years of support and an option for a third year, but after Broadcom acquired VMware and shifted to subscription-only models, it refused T-Mobile's $5.28 million renewal request. T-Mobile operates tens of thousands of virtual machines across approximately 303,140 CPU cores and is actively migrating off VMware, though moving over 1,000 applications presents significant technical challenges. A judge granted T-Mobile a temporary injunction for support through August 2026. T-Mobile even offered $20 million for two years of continued support. Broadcom claims it has spent $24 million providing support to T-Mobile, though T-Mobile disputes the scope of services actually used. T…
stories.opinion: This case exemplifies the serious risks of vendor lock-in and what happens when an acquiring company fundamentally changes the business model of a product that enterprises deeply depend on. Broadcom's argument that 'thousands have successfully migrated to subscription' rings hollow — many organizations likely capitulated not because the new terms were fair, but because they couldn't afford to fight. T-Mobile's situation, with tens of thousands of VMs and over 1,000 applications to migrate, show…

stories.title: Rapid spread of AI may worsen global inequality, UN warns
stories.summary: A new United Nations report warns that the rapid and uneven spread of artificial intelligence could worsen global inequality. The report proposes a shared framework for responsible AI development. UN Secretary-General António Guterres emphasized that without shared rules, governments and people will have less say in AI outcomes, as adoption and investment in the technology accelerate unevenly across the world.
stories.opinion: This article highlights a critically important issue that deserves widespread attention. The concern that AI could deepen global inequality is well-founded, as wealthier nations and corporations are far better positioned to develop and benefit from AI, while developing countries risk being left further behind. The UN's call for a shared framework is a reasonable and necessary step, though the challenge will be in implementation and enforcement. The article itself is brief and appears to be a tr…

stories.title: SpaceX has an AI device prototype, and it sure sounds phone-ish
stories.summary: SpaceX has shown investors a prototype of a sleek, 'handset-like' AI device ahead of going public, though Elon Musk has denied the reports. The device is reportedly slimmer than an iPhone and would run a proprietary operating system integrated with xAI technology, which SpaceX acquired earlier in 2026. The move aligns with SpaceX's apparent interest in expanding into wireless via Starlink Mobile. The article draws parallels to OpenAI's collaboration with Jony Ive on a competing AI device, and notes the failures of previous AI hardware attempts like Humane and Rabbit. It remains unclear whether SpaceX will mass-produce the device or if it's still exploratory.
stories.opinion: This is a fascinating but highly speculative development. SpaceX entering the consumer device market would be a bold move, and the combination of Starlink connectivity with an xAI-powered device could theoretically offer something genuinely differentiated — a phone-like device with native satellite connectivity and AI-first interfaces. However, there are major reasons for skepticism. The AI device graveyard is real (Humane, Rabbit), and building a compelling consumer electronics product is extr…

stories.title: Ashton Kutcher leaving Sound Ventures to launch new VC firm with Morgan Beller
stories.summary: Ashton Kutcher is leaving Sound Ventures, the VC firm he co-founded with Guy Oseary 11 years ago, to launch a new venture capital firm with Morgan Beller, formerly of NFX and Andreessen Horowitz. The new firm will focus on early-stage investments in AI infrastructure, energy, and deep tech startups, contrasting with Sound Ventures' approach of backing more established, category-leading AI companies. Kutcher's departure was partly driven by differing views on which startup stages to target. Sound Ventures, known for early investments in OpenAI, Anthropic, and World Labs, is not underperforming. Kutcher will remain an adviser to Sound, while Oseary and Sound GP Effie Epstein will advise the new firm. Beller previously co-led Meta's Libra cryptocurrency project.
stories.opinion: This is a strategically interesting move that reflects a broader shift in AI investing. As the major AI labs become increasingly expensive and established, the real opportunity may indeed be in the infrastructure layer — energy, hardware, and deep tech — that powers them. Kutcher has proven himself a surprisingly capable investor over the years, and pairing with Morgan Beller, who brings serious crypto, platform, and VC experience, creates a compelling combination. The amicable nature of the sp…

tweet_headlines.author: rauchg
tweet_headlines.headline: Guillermo Rauch says __v0.dev is the new npm and the new GitHub__ — you need instructions and best practices now, not repos to clone.
tweet_headlines.source_text:
https://t.co/pYz1Gn97jD is the new npm and the new github.

Over time, you need fewer 'templates' and large piles of code. I haven't cloned a repo in a very long time.

You need the instructions and best practices on how to build the best things.

tweet_headlines.author: phosphenq
tweet_headlines.headline: Google makes the case that MCP vs API is __the wrong fight__ — APIs aren't dying, they're being rebuilt so models can use software directly.
tweet_headlines.source_text:
Everyone in AI is arguing MCP vs API. Google just said that's the wrong fight.

APIs aren't dying. They're being rebuilt so that models, not just humans, can actually use software.

Here's the part that reframed it for me:
